source_metadata.title_raw: Daniel G. Sodergren - A-19-156 - August 6, 2019 - Pleasanton

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Daniel G. Sodergren 
City Attorney of Pleasanton 
PO Box 520 
Pleasanton, CA 94566-0802 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-156 
Dear ML Sodergren: 
August 6, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any ~dvice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
May a member of the City Council take part in decisions pertaining to a "Downtown 
Specific Plan Update" that will guide downtown preservation and development for more than 
twenty years, given that the councilmember owns residential real property within 500 feet of the 
Downtown Specific Plan boundaries? 
· 
CONCLUSION 
No. The project-which is wide in scope and includes numerous.changes and improvements 
to the downtown area-would have a foreseeable, material, and unique financial effect on the 
councilmember's real property located close to the project area. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
FileNo. A-19-156 
Page No. 2 
The City of Pleasanton ("City") has a "Downtown Specific Plan" ("DSP") that was adopted 
in 2002. The DSP is intended to serve as the primary regulatory guide for preserving and enhancing 
the 308-acre Downtown area. The City is preparing. to comprehensively update the DSP ("DSP 
Update"), as well as make related General Plan and zoning text and map amendments stemming 
from the DSP Update. 
The DSP Update is intended to guide the preservation and development of land within the 
Downtown area through the year 2040. The DSP Update will guide the location and distribution of 
land uses and public facilities, as well as policy and implementation measures pertaining to land 
use, transportation, parking, historic preservation, downtown design, infrastructure, and economic 
vitality. The City has formed a DSP Task Force that will make recommendations to the City's 
Planning Commission and City Council regarding the DSP Update. The DSP Update will then be 
considered by the City Council for adoption. 
Julie Testa is a member of the Pleasanton City Council. She has an ownership interest in her 
home located in the City. There are two ways to access Councilmember Testa's home: from 
Monaco Drive as it intersects with Sunol Boulevard, and at Puerto Vallarta as it intersects with 
Bernal A venue. A portion of First Street, which is in tl}.e southeastern boundary of the DSP Update, 
is located within 500 feet of Councilmember Testa's home. Other areas within the boundary of the 
DSP Update are located between 500 feet and 1,000 feet of Councilmember Testa's home. 
An Environmental Impact Report ("EIR") was prepared for the DSP Update pursuant to the 
California Environmental Quality Act. The EIR concluded that the DSP Update will have less than 
significant visual and noise impacts and does not require mitigation measures. The EIR concluded 
that the DSP Update would have significant regional impacts on air quality, but did not identify any 
unique air quality impacts that could affect.Councilmember Testa's home. The EIR did not identify 
any impacts to the traffic on the street where Councilmember Testa's home is located. The EIR also 
concluded that there may be potential impacts to Sunol Boulevard and Bernal Avenue, but the 
impacts would be less than significant and would not require mitigation measures. 
You enclosed a map with your request for advice that, you state, "shows that approximately 
25% of property within City limits that has a residential land use designation in the City's General 
Plan is located within a 5,000-foot radius of the boundaries of the DSP Update." You also cited 
several documents in your request for advice and provided links to those documents, which are 
currently available to the public online. Those documents include a draft of the DSP Update, a draft 
of the EIR, and "Findings of Fact and Statement of Overriding Considerations." Those documents 
include information regarding specific development plans, including "two new land use 
designations, creation. of an active ground floor overlay, development of a concept plan for the civic 
center area, and a series of streetscape enhancements." (Draft D_SP Update, p. 1.) 
ANALYSIS 
S.ection 87100 provides, "[n]o public official at any level of state or local government shall 
make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a 


File No. A-19-156 
Page No. 3 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest." "A 
public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from 
its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on 
certain enumerated economic interests, including "[ a ]ny real property in which the public official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more." (Section 87103(b).) 
Councilmember Testa has an ownership in her home, which is presumably worth more than $2,000. 
Therefore, she has an economic interest in her real property. 
A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if 
the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent 
extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably 
foreseeable. (Regulation 18701.) The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental 
decision on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial interest, other than a 
leasehold interest, is material whenever the governmental decision "[i]nvolves property located 500 
feet or less from the property line of the parcel unless there is clear and convincing evidence •that 
the decision will not have any measurable impact on the official's property .... " (Regulation 
18702.2(a)(7).) 
Councilmember Testa's home is located less than 500 feet from the DSP that would guide 
the preservation and development of Pleasanton's downtown area for the next twenty-plus years. 
The DSP Update will guide the location and distribution of land uses and public facilities, as well as 
policy and implementation measures pertaining to land use, transportation, parking, historic 
preservation, downtown design, infrastructure, and economic vitality. Although you note certain 
effects of the DSP Update, such as visual and noise impacts, have been deemed "less than 
significant" in the EIR2, you have not provided clear and convincing evidence to rebut the 
presumption that decisions pertaining to the DSP Update, including approval or rejection of the 
Update, would have a material financial effect on Councilmember Testa's home. As such, 
Councilmeinber Testa has a potentially disqualifying conflict of interest unless the decision's effect 
on her real property interest is indistinguishable from the effect the DSP Update decision(s) would 
have on the public generally. (See Sections 18700, 18703.) 
A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial interest is 
indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official establishes that a significant 
segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her financial interest is not unique 
compared to the effect on the significant segment. (Regulation 18703(a).) A "significant segment of 
the public" includes at least 25 percent of all real property or residential real property within the 
official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b )(2).) A "unique effect" on a public official's financial 
interest, in tum, includes a disproportionate effect on an official's real property resulting from the 
proximity of a project that is the subject of a decision. (Regulation 18703(c)(3).) In this case, it is 
2 The EIR explains that "[a] 'less than significant impact' would cause a minor change in the physical environment but 
the impact would not meet or exceed the significance threshold," and does not require mitigation. Additionally, "[a] 
'less than significant with mitigation' impact would have a substantial adverse effect on the physical environment, but 
can be reduced to a less-than-significant level with mitigation." Lastly, "[a] 'significant and unavoidable impact' would 
cause a substantial adverse effect on the environment, and no known feasible mitigation measures are available to 
reduce the impact to a less-than-significant level." (See Draft EIR, p. 3-4.) 


File No. A-19-156 
Page No. 4 
reasonable to expect that the DSP Update, which includes various development plans for the 
downtown area would have a greater impact on the market value of real property within 500 feet of 
its boundaries, compared to real property 5,000 feet (nearly one mile) away. For instance, the DSP 
Update includes numerous planned "streetscape enhancements," which one would reasonably 
expect to have a greater financial effect on the real property within and immediately surrounding 
the downtown area. As the draft of the DSP Update states, "[m]aintaining and enhancing the 
character of the downtown is a community priority and will strengthen its appeal and economic 
vitality." (Draft DSP Update, p. 19.) It stands to reason that a more appealing and economically 
strong downtown area would also have a strong impact on the residences immediately surrounding 
it, i.e., those properties that could most readily enjoy the improvements to the downtown area, with 
diminishing financial effects on real property further away. 
Additionally, you noted that there may be potential impacts to traffic on Sunol Boulevard 
and Bernal A venue. Although the EIR deemed the impacts to be less than "significant" (based on 
the EIR criteria with respect to the foreseen environmental impact), any impact would have a 
greater foreseeable effect on real property around the comer from that intersection, as opposed to 
property approximately one mile away. The same is true of potential impacts to traffic at the 
intersection of Bernal A venue and Main Street, which is within 1000 feet of Councilmember 
Testa's real property and may be significantly impacted without mitigation measures. (See Findings 
of Fact and Statement of Overriding Considerations, pp. 12, 14.) 
Due to its close proximity to the downtown area subject to the DSP Update's numerous 
planned enhancements, the reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on Councilmember 
Testa's real property is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. Therefore, 
Councilmember Testa has a financial interest in governmental decisions pertaining to the Update 
and is disqualified from taking part in those decisions. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
KMC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division

·

No. The project-which is wide in scope and includes numerous.changes and improvements 
to the downtown area-would have a foreseeable, material, and unique financial effect on the 
councilmember's real property located close to the project area.

·
